mp_soba_kitayama_gonbe.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. mp_tonga.vegan_detail: A pork-cutlet and Western meat-dish specialist; demi-glace and dashi-based seasonings are animal-derived. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented and the concept offers no genuine vegan path.

mp_katsuzen.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is pork dredged in wheat flour and panko breadcrumbs then deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce and accompanying teishoku items (miso soup, soy-based seasonings) are wheat- and soy-heavy with shared frying oil. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.
mp_katsuzen.vegan_detail: A pork-cutlet specialist; core menu is deep-fried pork. No vegan or vegetarian options documented. Dashi and frying oil make cross-contamination likely. Not suitable for vegans.

mp_tonkatsu_shimizu.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is breaded (wheat flour, panko) and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce and the izakaya menu are soy- and wheat-heavy. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.
mp_tonkatsu_shimizu.vegan_detail: A pork-cutlet specialist; the signature items are katsu sandwiches and tonkatsu. No vegan options indicated and no genuine vegan potential.

mp_katsukatsu_tonton.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is breaded (wheat panko + flour) and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce, miso soup (dashi/soy), and pickles all typically contain wheat/soy. Shared fryer means cross-contamination. Unsuitable for celiac diners.
mp_katsukatsu_tonton.vegan_detail: A pork-cutlet/bento specialist; core menu is deep-fried pork with dashi-based sides (miso soup, barley rice, cabbage, pickles). No documented vegan options; effectively not vegan-friendly.

mp_masuya.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu (hamburg steak with demi-glace, omurice/ketchup rice, Napolitan, shrimp fry, dashimaki) is built on wheat flour (roux/breading), soy-based sauces, and Japanese dashi. No gluten-free menu or allergen handling indicated. A celiac visitor should avoid; cross-contamination is essentially certain in a tiny 6-seat kitchen.
mp_masuya.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Menu centers on meat, eggs, seafood, demi-glace and dashi; even the rice dishes use egg and meat-based sauces. No documented plant-based dishes.

mp_grill_fukuya_coffee_gril.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu (hamburg, omelet rice/Fukuya Rice, Western cutlets) is wheat- and soy-heavy: ketchup rice, demi-glace/sauces, fried items and bread all typically involve wheat flour and soy-based seasonings. No documented gluten-free preparation. Assume not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
mp_grill_fukuya_coffee_gril.vegan_detail: This is an egg-, meat-, and dairy-centric yoshoku shop; the signature Fukuya Rice is built on egg and ketchup rice (often with ham), and mains are hamburg/omelets. No documented vegan options; genuine vegan dining is not realistic here.

mp_kitchen_papa.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu (hand-kneaded hamburger, demi-glace sauce, fried shrimp/chicken nanban) is wheat- and soy-heavy: demi-glace and fried items use flour, and Japanese yoshoku seasonings rely on soy and dashi. No gluten-free accommodation documented. Foreign guests with gluten restrictions should not assume safety.
mp_kitchen_papa.vegan_detail: The restaurant's identity is centered on meat (hamburger, fried meat/seafood) and freshly milled white rice with non-vegan sauces (demi-glace). No vegan menu indicated. Rice itself is vegan but there is no documented standalone vegan meal; treat as not vegan-friendly.

mp_grill_yamamoto.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature items (ハンバーグ with bread-crumb binder and demi-glace/roux sauces, クリームコロッケ which is breaded and fried, homemade sauces and dressings) are wheat-heavy, and soy sauce/flour-based roux are typical of 洋食. No gluten-free accommodation indicated; should not be considered safe for celiac diners.
mp_grill_yamamoto.vegan_detail: Traditional Japanese 洋食 specializing in hamburg steak and cream croquettes; dishes are meat-, dairy-, and likely dashi/butter-heavy. No indication of any vegan offerings. A foreign vegan visitor would have essentially nothing suitable here.

mp_coffee_grill_fukuya.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is classic yoshoku — hamburg steak, omurice, fried shrimp, tonkatsu, cream croquettes, sandwiches — heavily reliant on wheat (breading, demi-glace roux, bread), soy sauce, and dashi. No documented gluten-free preparation. Assume cross-contamination throughout.
mp_coffee_grill_fukuya.vegan_detail: Menu is centered on meat, egg, seafood and dairy (omurice, hamburg, croquettes). No documented vegan options; dashi and animal fats are pervasive in yoshoku. Not a viable vegan destination.

mp_imai_shokudo.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature saba-ni is simmered in a soy-sauce / sake / mirin / sugar tare (soy sauce contains wheat), and teishoku meals are dashi- and soy-heavy. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.
mp_imai_shokudo.vegan_detail: Core menu is fish-based (mackerel) with dashi and soy seasonings throughout; no documented vegan options at this small set-meal diner.

mp_ofuku.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is wheat-flour udon (and soba) in a kombu/mackerel (鯖) dashi; soy-based seasoning and wheat are central. No gluten-free accommodation indicated. A foreign guest with celiac disease should avoid this shop.
mp_ofuku.vegan_detail: The signature broth is made from kombu AND mackerel (鯖), so even plain udon is not vegan. No vegan menu or plant-based dashi indicated. The grilled-mochi udon uses the same fish dashi. Not suitable for vegans.

mp_seiyoken.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a wheat-noodle (中華そば/ramen) and gyoza shop; ramen, gyoza wrappers, and likely soy-sauce-based broths/seasonings contain wheat and soy. No gluten-free accommodation indicated. A celiac visitor should treat this as unsafe.
mp_seiyoken.vegan_detail: No vegan offerings. Signature dishes (白梅麺 with simmered pork, 紅梅麺 with chicken, gyoza, Chinese noodle dishes) are meat- and animal-based, with dashi/soy-sauce broths. No documented plant-based menu.

mp_yuraku_saikan.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a wheat/soy/dashi-heavy town-Chinese menu — ramen/中華そば, fried rice, ankake yakisoba, mapo tofu, tori-ten all rely on wheat noodles/flour, soy sauce, and shared woks/fryers. No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Treat as unsuitable for strict gluten avoidance.
mp_yuraku_saikan.vegan_detail: No vegan offering. Menu is meat- and animal-based (chicken, pork, egg fried rice, meat sauces) with soy-sauce/dashi seasoning and shared cooking surfaces. No documented vegetarian/vegan options.

mp_maruse.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. The core dish is 中華そば — wheat-based ramen noodles in a soy- and dashi-based broth (蒲鉾/kamaboko fishcake also typically contains wheat starch). No gluten-free accommodation indicated. Treat as unsafe for celiac diners.
mp_maruse.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Signature bowl features 豚バラチャーシュー (pork belly), pork/fish-based broth, and 蒲鉾 (fishcake). No plant-based menu or substitutions documented; broth is animal-based throughout.

mp_rokuhara_hanten.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic Chinese diner whose dishes (ramen, gyoza, fried rice, ankake bowls, the signature 六波羅丼) rely heavily on wheat-based noodles/wrappers, soy sauce, and shared woks/fryers. No gluten-free accommodation documented; cross-contamination is essentially unavoidable.
mp_rokuhara_hanten.vegan_detail: No vegan offering. Menu is meat- and egg-centric (karaage, pork, egg-thickened dishes) and broths/sauces are animal/dashi-based. No documented vegan options.

mp_gyoza_taizo.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. Gyoza wrappers are wheat-flour based and the signature miso dipping sauce plus typical seasonings involve soy/wheat. No indication of GF accommodation; assume cross-contamination in a small kitchen.
mp_gyoza_taizo.vegan_detail: The core product is meat gyoza (Kagoshima black-pork mince). No vegan items documented. Not suitable for vegans.

mp_sanpo_no_gyoza.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour gyoza wrappers (皮から手作り = skins made from scratch from wheat flour); dishes also involve soy sauce and typical Chinese seasonings. No gluten-free accommodation documented. Avoid if gluten-intolerant.
mp_sanpo_no_gyoza.vegan_detail: No vegan offering documented. Gyoza, Chinese dishes and ramen are meat/animal-based; gyoza commonly contain pork and the broths use animal stock. No evidence of vegan menu items.

mp_mr_gyoza_mister_gyoza.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a gyoza (dumpling) specialty shop — the wrappers are wheat flour and the menu centers on gyoza, ramen (wheat noodles), and karaage (typically flour-dredged). Seasonings are soy-based. No gluten-free options are advertised; high cross-contamination risk. Not suitable for celiac diners.
mp_mr_gyoza_mister_gyoza.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options advertised. Signature gyoza contain pork/meat filling and the menu is meat- and broth-centric (ramen, karaage). No documented plant-based dish; vegans should not rely on this venue.

mp_kaminari_enmachi.gf_detail: Charcoal yakitori. Tare-glazed skewers contain wheat soy sauce; salt-grilled (shio) skewers avoid the tare but share a grill. Not celiac-safe.
mp_kaminari_enmachi.vegan_detail: Grilled chicken skewers over binchōtan. No vegan options.

mp_jikaseimen_tengu.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. The shop centers on udon and soba (wheat and buckwheat noodles) and homemade wheat noodles; broths typically use soy sauce and dashi. High wheat and soy content — unsuitable for celiac diners.
mp_jikaseimen_tengu.vegan_detail: No vegan menu confirmed. Japanese noodle dishes here almost certainly use dashi (commonly bonito/fish-based) and may include meat/egg toppings; a vegan diner would need to ask directly, and even plain noodles risk fish-based broth.

mp_mendokoro_mimatsu.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat udon noodles; dashi is made from 昆布(kombu)・鰹(bonito)・いりこ/niboshi (dried small fish) and dishes use soy-based seasonings. Wheat and fish/dashi are central; no dedicated gluten-free preparation. Cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable.
mp_mendokoro_mimatsu.vegan_detail: No vegan options. Dashi is animal-derived (bonito/niboshi), oden and menu items rely on fish-based stock, and no plant-based broth is mentioned. Not suitable for vegans/vegetarians.

mp_honkaku_teuchi_udon_taig.gf_detail: NOT celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour handmade udon; the kitchen handles flour throughout and tempura is served, so cross-contact is unavoidable. Dashi-based broths and soy-based seasonings (soy sauce/tsuyu) are standard. No gluten-free menu confirmed.
mp_honkaku_teuchi_udon_taig.vegan_detail: No vegan menu confirmed. Udon broth/tsuyu is typically made with bonito/fish (and often kombu) dashi, and signature items include chicken, shrimp, and chikuwa tempura. A vegan would need to ask directly; no plant-based dashi option is documented.

mp_rokujo_shinmachi_shofuku.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is wheat-based (udon, donburi) and soba/茶そば is typically blended with wheat flour. Dishes rely on soy-sauce and dashi-based broths (soy + fish dashi). No gluten-free preparation indicated.
mp_rokujo_shinmachi_shofuku.vegan_detail: Soba/udon broths and donburi use fish-based dashi (katsuo/kombu blends) and the signature dishes include カツ丼 and 親子丼 (pork/chicken/egg). No vegan menu indicated; even noodle broth is not vegan.

mp_inoue_no_gyoza.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is gyoza (wheat-flour wrappers) plus ramen (wheat noodles) and Chinese dishes; soy sauce and likely pork/dashi-based seasonings are pervasive. No documented gluten-free accommodations.
mp_inoue_no_gyoza.vegan_detail: No documented vegan options. Gyoza, ramen, and Chinese dishes here are meat-based (pork) with soy/animal-based seasonings; no plant-based menu indicated.

mp_ryuen_gyoza_no_mise_ryue.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour gyoza (the shop is famous for its house-made/hand-stretched wheat skins), plus ramen, fried rice and Tianjin dishes; soy sauce and vinegar-soy dipping are standard. Heavy wheat and soy exposure; no gluten-free accommodation documented.
mp_ryuen_gyoza_no_mise_ryue.vegan_detail: Gyoza filling is described as light and vegetable-centered, but no confirmation it is meat-free, and Chinese dishes/ramen typically use pork/meat broth. No vegan menu documented; a vegan would need to ask directly. Treat as not confirmed vegan.

mp_soba_roujina.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The core product is soba; even juwari (100% buckwheat) noodles are prepared in a kitchen handling wheat, and the dipping sauce (そばつゆ) is dashi-based, typically containing soy sauce (wheat + soy) and bonito dashi. No gluten-free or dedicated allergen-safe preparation is documented. Cross-contamination from wheat is likely.
mp_soba_roujina.vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Soba is typically served with a katsuo/kombu dashi-based tsuyu (bonito = non-vegan), and the menu features items such as duck (鴨) and izakaya-style dishes. A vegan would need to ask directly; plain zaru soba might be possible only if the dashi-based sauce is omitted, which is not confirmed.

mp_yamaga_echizen_soba_yama.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is soba; many shops cut buckwheat with wheat flour (二八), and tempura, ソースカツ丼, and soy-based つゆ/dashi all contain wheat and soy. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation is advertised. Cross-contamination from shared broth and frying is highly likely.
mp_yamaga_echizen_soba_yama.vegan_detail: No vegan offering advertised. Soba broth (つゆ) is dashi-based, typically using 鰹節 (bonito); listed dishes feature bonito flakes, sauce pork cutlet, grilled mackerel sushi, and tempura. A vegan would need to ask directly, and even plain soba is likely served with fish-dashi tsuyu, so options are minimal. Default to "ask"; realistically limited.

mp_rokuyosha_chikaten_rokuy.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The signature items are homemade donuts (wheat flour) and the shop is a coffee/café; no gluten-free menu is documented. Wheat-based pastries are central. Coffee itself is naturally GF but shared-kitchen wheat handling makes celiac-safe assurance impossible. Treat as not suitable for strict celiacs without direct confirmation.
mp_rokuyosha_chikaten_rokuy.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Black self-roasted hand-drip coffee is plant-based; however the famous donuts contain egg/dairy and milk is offered for coffee. No confirmed vegan food options — a vegan visitor should ask, and realistically only black coffee is reliably suitable.

mp_tsukiji.gf_detail: グルテンフリー対応の記載なし。クラシック喫茶であり軽食・洋菓子等は小麦使用が前提。ウィンナー珈琲自体は珈琲＋ホイップクリームだが、店としてコンタミ管理やグルテンフリー保証はなく、セリアック患者には安全とは言えない。
mp_tsukiji.vegan_detail: ヴィーガン対応の明示なし。看板のウィンナー珈琲は乳製品（ホイップクリーム）を使用。ブラックコーヒー等は植物性で飲める可能性があるが、ミルク不使用の可否は要確認。

mp_coffee_cattleya_coffee_k.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. A retro kissaten centered on coffee and sandwiches; sandwiches and toast use wheat bread, and typical menu items (sandwiches, cream soda, sweets) are not gluten-free. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Cross-contamination with wheat is highly likely. A celiac should avoid food items here; black coffee itself is plausibly fine but unverified.
mp_coffee_cattleya_coffee_k.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling is documented. Black coffee (brewed with sacred-water-vein water per sources) and some drinks may be vegan, but signature items like sandwiches and cream soda contain egg/dairy. A vegan would need to ask in person; nothing confirmed.

mp_jambo.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki and yakisoba are wheat-flour based (batter and wheat noodles), and are typically seasoned with soy-sauce-based sauce and often dashi — all wheat/soy/dashi-heavy. No gluten-free preparation is indicated on the page.
mp_jambo.vegan_detail: No vegan information on the page. Okonomiyaki and yakisoba at such shops standardly contain egg, pork/meat, and bonito-based dashi/toppings, plus okonomiyaki sauce. No vegan options indicated; treat as not vegan-friendly.

mp_karasu.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki and yakisoba are wheat-flour-based; the batter and dishes use bonito-kombu dashi and chicken dashi, and sauces blend Kyoto/Osaka/Hiroshima okonomiyaki sauces (soy- and wheat-containing). Spicy items use doubanjiang/yanyanjiang (soy/wheat). Heavy wheat, soy, and dashi presence throughout. No gluten-free accommodation documented.
mp_karasu.vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. Menu is centered on pork okonomiyaki, beef tendon, and other meat/seafood teppanyaki items, with dashi (bonito-kombu, chicken) used as a base. A vegan would need to ask directly, and given the dashi-based batter, suitable options are unlikely without special accommodation.

mp_yorimichi_okonomiyaki_yo.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Okonomiyaki/beta-yaki and manbo-yaki use a wheat-flour batter; the standard sauce is soy/Worcester-based and the batter/broth is dashi-heavy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented and cross-contamination on a shared teppan is likely.
mp_yorimichi_okonomiyaki_yo.vegan_detail: Not vegan. Core dishes contain egg in the batter, pork/meat and bonito-based dashi/sauce are standard for beta-yaki and hoso-yaki. No vegan menu is documented; a foreign vegan diner could not be reliably accommodated.

mp_kyoto_shitamachi_no_okon.gf_detail: NOT celiac-safe. ベタ焼き/お好み焼きは小麦粉ベースの生地が中核で、ソースには小麦・大豆（醤油由来）、出汁も使用される。グルテン・小麦・大豆・出汁を多用するため、セリアック対応は不可。専用のグルテンフリー対応情報なし。
mp_kyoto_shitamachi_no_okon.vegan_detail: ヴィーガン対応の明示情報なし。お好み焼き/ベタ焼きは通常、卵・豚肉・魚介・かつお出汁を含み、焼肉メニューもある。植物性のみの選択肢は確認できず、対応可否は要問い合わせ。デフォルトではヴィーガン非対応と推定。

mp_betayaki_setsu_chan.gf_detail: お好み焼き・ベタ焼きは小麦粉生地が主体で、ソースには小麦・大豆が、出汁にも魚介系（鰹節等）が含まれるのが通例。グルテン除去メニューの記載はなく、セリアック対応は不可と判断すべき。
mp_betayaki_setsu_chan.vegan_detail: 鉄板お好み焼き店で、生地に卵、具材に豚・イカ等、トッピングに鰹節・卵を用いるのが標準。ヴィーガン対応メニューの明記なし。卵・乳・動物性出汁を避けた対応可否は来店時に直接確認が必要。

mp_sho_miso.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The cuisine is centered on miso (made from soybeans, and many miso varieties also contain wheat/barley koji), and the oden broth is dashi-heavy (described variously as chicken-bone + kombu dashi, or kombu + bonito dashi). The signature dish is generously topped with white miso, and the menu includes 生麩 (fresh wheat gluten) dengaku. Heavy soy, dashi, and wheat-gluten exposure means it is unsuitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
mp_sho_miso.vegan_detail: Several plant-based items exist (daikon, atsuage/fried tofu, mushrooms, lotus root, rolled Chinese cabbage), but the oden broth uses animal-derived dashi (chicken bone and/or bonito), so dishes are not strictly vegan as served. A vegan would need to ask whether any items can be prepared without the meat/fish dashi.

mp_daruma_to_kingyo.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is an oden specialist: oden simmers ingredients in a dashi broth typically built on soy sauce (wheat-containing) and bonito/kombu dashi, and many oden items (chikuwa, hanpen, satsuma-age, fried items) contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or labeling is documented. A celiac should not assume safety here.
mp_daruma_to_kingyo.vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian menu is documented. Oden broth is overwhelmingly dashi-based (bonito/fish stock) and seasoned with soy, so even vegetable oden items are typically cooked in animal-derived broth. Some vegetable dishes exist (e.g., the signature potato salad uses egg and oden broth), but nothing is confirmed plant-only. A strict vegan would need to ask directly and likely find few or no compliant options.

mp_karasemitei.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is wheat-flour-dredged and panko (wheat breadcrumb) coated pork cutlet; sauces and the izakaya/Western menu typically involve soy sauce and dashi. Heavy wheat and soy exposure with high cross-contamination risk in shared frying. No gluten-free accommodation documented.
mp_karasemitei.vegan_detail: Core menu is pork tonkatsu. Editorial coverage notes a bagna cauda appetizer made with organic Kyoto vegetables and use of rapeseed oil rather than lard, but bagna cauda traditionally contains anchovy/dairy, so it is not reliably vegan. No vegan menu is advertised; a vegan diner would need to ask directly, and options would at best be very limited.

mp_jukuseibuta_kawamura.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is breaded with wheat panko (糖度低めのパン粉 from Tokyo's 中屋パン粉工場) and deep-fried; accompanying sauces are soy-based (屋久島あごだし醤油, おぢかソース). Shared fryer and wheat/soy throughout. No gluten-free options indicated.
mp_jukuseibuta_kawamura.vegan_detail: A tonkatsu specialist centered entirely on aged pork cutlets; no vegan or vegetarian menu items indicated. Frying uses lard (and lard/canola blend per reports). Not suitable for vegans/vegetarians.

mp_tonkatsu_yamamoto.gf_detail: Not gluten-free / not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is pork breaded in wheat-flour panko and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce and accompaniments typically contain wheat and soy. High cross-contamination risk in a small shared kitchen. No gluten-free accommodation indicated.
mp_tonkatsu_yamamoto.vegan_detail: Core menu is deep-fried pork cutlet (tonkatsu); the shop is built around pork. No vegan options indicated and the broader Japanese/Western menu likely uses dashi, egg, and meat. Not suitable for vegans.

mp_tonkatsu_dokoro_katsuhir.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tonkatsu is breaded (wheat panko + wheat flour batter) and deep-fried; tonkatsu sauce contains wheat and soy; accompaniments (rice, miso soup, curry) involve soy/dashi. Shared fryer means cross-contamination with wheat. No gluten-free offering indicated.
mp_tonkatsu_dokoro_katsuhir.vegan_detail: A tonkatsu specialist whose core menu is breaded deep-fried pork; sides and sauces use animal-derived and dashi-based ingredients. No vegan options indicated on any source.

mp_bonjour.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Menu centers on yoshoku hambagu and steak, which typically rely on wheat (flour binder/breadcrumbs, demi-glace sauce), soy sauce, and dashi-based seasonings. No gluten-free accommodation is indicated on the page; cross-contamination likely.
mp_bonjour.vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. This is a meat-focused Western-style restaurant (hamburg steak, beef steak); dishes use animal protein, dairy, and meat-based sauces. The page lists no plant-based menu items.

mp_yoshoku_ogata.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. As a 洋食 (Japanese-Western) restaurant, dishes rely heavily on wheat-based flour, breading, demi-glace/roux sauces, and likely soy and dashi-based seasonings; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.
mp_yoshoku_ogata.vegan_detail: The menu centers on premium beef (hamburger, meat dishes) and French-technique meat/dairy preparations; no vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and dairy/eggs/meat stocks are integral to 洋食 cooking.

mp_yoshoku_no_mise_mishina.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a classic yoshoku/fry-set restaurant: signature dishes are crab cream croquettes, shrimp/fry sets, and beef stew — all built on wheat (breading/batter, roux-based brown sauce, flour-thickened cream). No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Cross-contamination from a small shared fry kitchen is highly likely.
mp_yoshoku_no_mise_mishina.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. The menu centers on meat (beef stew), seafood (shrimp, crab croquettes) and likely dairy/egg in cream-based and breaded dishes. A vegan diner would need to ask directly and should not expect dedicated options; potage and side vegetables may contain meat/dairy/dashi.

mp_donryu_yodo_ten_tonryu_y.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. A 町中華 menu centered on gyoza, ramen, fried rice, stir-fries, and set meals relies heavily on wheat (gyoza wrappers, ramen noodles), soy sauce, and likely chicken/pork-based dashi and shared frying oil. No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Assume cross-contamination throughout.
mp_donryu_yodo_ten_tonryu_y.vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian options are documented on any source. Dishes described are meat- and seafood-heavy (fried chicken, shrimp tempura, crab omelette, fried dumplings), with vegetable stir-fries that still use meat-based broth/soy/oils. A vegetable dish might be requested, but a strict vegan must ask directly; no confirmed plant-based offerings.

mp_marui_shokudo.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is wheat-based ramen/udon noodles, breaded (flour) fried dishes, and soy-sauce/dashi-based broths and seasonings; no gluten-free accommodation indicated. High cross-contamination risk.
mp_marui_shokudo.vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. Menu centers on meat/fish (charsiu, pork cutlet, breaded fish, chicken) and broths/sauces almost certainly made with animal-based dashi (katsuo/niboshi) or pork. No documented plant-based or dashi-free dishes.

mp_marushin_hanten.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core menu is gyoza (wheat dumpling wrappers), ramen (wheat noodles), tenshinhan and other Chinese dishes relying on soy sauce and wheat/soy-based seasonings; heavy gluten and soy exposure with high cross-contamination risk. No gluten-free accommodation documented.
mp_marushin_hanten.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The signature gyoza use aged pork; Chinese dishes typically use meat, egg, and pork/chicken-based stocks. A vegan would need to ask on-site, and options are unlikely. No labeled plant-based items confirmed.

mp_tetsunabe_gyoza_to_osake.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Gyoza wrappers are hand-kneaded wheat dough; fillings use pork and Chinese spices, and an izakaya menu typically involves soy sauce and dashi. No gluten-free options or allergen program documented.
mp_tetsunabe_gyoza_to_osake.vegan_detail: Official site explicitly states pork is used in all gyoza. As an izakaya focused on iron-pan pork gyoza, no vegan or vegetarian gyoza/options are documented.

mp_teuchi_udon_kasugai.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Core product is wheat-flour handmade udon; tempura is wheat-battered; dashi/broths and curry udon are very likely to contain soy sauce, wheat, and bonito/dashi. No gluten-free menu or dedicated preparation indicated. A celiac/gluten-sensitive diner should avoid.
mp_teuchi_udon_kasugai.vegan_detail: No vegan menu confirmed. Japanese udon broths typically use katsuo/dashi (bonito/fish stock), and tempura is fried; some items (zaru udon, kake) could potentially be made without meat but the broth is unlikely to be vegan. A vegan would need to ask directly about dashi; no documented vegan options.

mp_okazuya_ishikawa.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Cuisine is obanzai/Japanese home cooking built on dashi, soy sauce, miso and likely wheat-containing seasonings; no gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is advertised. Cross-contamination with soy/wheat should be assumed.
mp_okazuya_ishikawa.vegan_detail: Menu centers on seasonal-vegetable simmered dishes (~100 obanzai recipes), so plant-based dishes likely exist, but most are simmered in fish/bonito dashi and the genre is listed as 居酒屋・海鮮 (seafood). No vegan labeling is provided; a vegan diner would need to ask and confirm dashi/animal content per dish.

mp_roji_usagi.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. The cuisine is おばんざい/和食 built around dashi (fish/kombu stock), miso, soy sauce, pickles, and dishes like だし巻き (dashi egg roll) and 塩さば (salted mackerel) — dashi, soy and wheat-containing seasonings are pervasive. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented; not safe for celiacs.
mp_roji_usagi.vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented on the official site or Tabelog. Set meals are fish/meat-centered with vegetable おばんざい sides, but dashi and miso typically contain fish; sweets (parfait, anmitsu, sweet-potato ice cream) may suit vegetarians but not strict vegans. A vegan would need to ask directly; nothing confirmed.

mp_kyozakana_fukuya.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Menu centers on dashi-based broths, soy sauce, sabazushi (vinegared mackerel sushi), and tempura (wheat batter) — wheat- and soy-heavy throughout. No gluten-free menu or accommodation stated on official site or listings.
mp_kyozakana_fukuya.vegan_detail: Seafood-focused izakaya (sashimi, fish dishes, dashi broths). No vegan or vegetarian menu information found on the official site, Tabelog, or Hot Pepper. Dashi is typically fish/katsuobushi-based, so even vegetable dishes are likely not vegan.

mp_shunsai_imari.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Cuisine is dashi-heavy (fish-based broth in obanzai, miso soup) and soy-heavy (miso, soy sauce, pickles); wheat is likely present (soy sauce, possible tempura batter, miso variants). No gluten-free/allergen accommodation is published. Phone (075-231-1354) for any dietary needs; cross-contamination not addressable for celiac.
mp_shunsai_imari.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or labeling published. Most dishes use dashi (fish stock) and animal products; menu includes some plant-based-looking items (Dutch-style braised eggplant ¥500, simmered greens & yuba/tofu skin ¥500, corn tempura ¥580, potato salad ¥680) but these likely contain dashi and are not confirmed vegan. Breakfast set is fixed and includes fish, egg, miso. Vegans should call 075-231-1354 to confirm preparation.

mp_tempura_kyoboshi.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tempura is by definition a wheat-flour batter deep-fried preparation; cross-contamination in shared frying oil is inherent. Although the house "京星流" batter is described as egg-free with a thin coating, it still relies on wheat flour. Dipping is served with lemon juice, salt, and grated daikon (no flour-thickened tentsuyu), but the core dish remains wheat-based. Soy/dashi exposure also likely in an accompanying Japanese course. Avoid for gluten intolerance/celiac.
mp_tempura_kyoboshi.vegan_detail: A fixed reservation-only tempura course built around seafood and seasonal items; no vegan menu is advertised. Some vegetable tempura exists but the batter and shared frying oil, plus likely dashi/seafood elements, make a fully vegan meal unconfirmed. Would need to ask the restaurant directly in advance — and given the completely reservation-only kaiseki-style course format, accommodation is uncertain.

mp_tominokoji_tempura_matsu.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Tempura is wheat-flour battered and deep-fried, and the tentsuyu dipping sauce is dashi/soy-based (wheat-containing soy sauce). Cross-contamination in shared frying oil is highly likely. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
mp_tominokoji_tempura_matsu.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly as a whole: the menu is seafood-centric (live shrimp, fish) and the batter/dipping sauce rely on dashi (fish/bonito stock). Vegetable tempura exists but is fried in shared oil and served with dashi-based sauce, so it is not reliably vegan. A dedicated vegan would need to ask in advance; suitability is not confirmed.

mp_ichikawaya_coffee.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Signature menu items are fruit sandwiches on wheat bread (gluten) and baked goods; coffee shop with no advertised gluten-free menu. No verified GF accommodation found. A foreign student with celiac disease should avoid the bread items.
mp_ichikawaya_coffee.vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature fruit sandwiches use fresh dairy cream (生クリーム) and bread; coffee can be taken black. No confirmed plant-milk options found, so a vegan would need to ask on-site. Status set to 'ask' (unconfirmed).

mp_shimao_coffee.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Signature offerings are wheat-based (バタートースト, ケーキ) prepared in a shared kitchen; no gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented. Toast/cake-centric café — assume wheat exposure throughout.
mp_shimao_coffee.vegan_detail: No vegan/vegetarian options documented. The flagship morning plate contains bacon, fried egg, butter, and yogurt; cakes and toast use dairy/eggs. None of the fetched sources mention vegan or vegetarian accommodation. A vegan would need to ask, with low expectation of suitable options.

mp_baji_tofu.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. As a yakitori izakaya, the menu relies on a tare (sweet soy-based glaze) aged ~20 years, which contains wheat and soy; soy sauce, dashi, and shared grills are pervasive. Salt-only (塩) skewers reduce but do not eliminate cross-contamination and gluten exposure risk. No gluten-free program documented.
mp_baji_tofu.vegan_detail: A charcoal yakitori specialist centered on chicken (skewers from Tajima/Okayama birds); no vegan menu or documented plant-based options. Dashi and animal-based seasonings are standard. Vegans would have effectively nothing reliable here.

mp_kuchibashi_modan.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. This is a yakitori (grilled chicken skewer) restaurant; standard yakitori preparation relies on a soy-sauce-and-wheat-based tare glaze, and soy sauce itself typically contains wheat. Dashi and soy are heavily used. No gluten-free accommodation is documented on any page fetched. A celiac diner should not assume safety here.
mp_kuchibashi_modan.vegan_detail: Poultry-focused omakase yakitori counter using 京赤地鶏 and フレッシュなひね鶏. No vegan or vegetarian menu items are documented on any source fetched (Tabelog, Gnavi, Hitosara). A plant-based diner is not accommodated.

mp_torisaki.gf_detail: Not celiac-safe. Yakitori is glazed/seasoned with tare (a soy-sauce-based sauce containing wheat) and the omakase relies on soy and dashi-based seasonings; cross-contact on shared grill is likely. Gluten-free cannot be assured.
mp_torisaki.vegan_detail: A reservation-only chicken yakitori omakase house; the entire fixed course centers on chicken and other animal products with no vegan course. Not suitable for vegans.

vm_kyoto_baisenya.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature tsukemen noodles contain wheat, and wheat is explicitly listed as an allergen for the dish. Whether the soy sauce/seasonings used are wheat-free is not confirmed. A celiac or gluten-avoiding diner should not rely on this venue without direct confirmation; the noodle dish is the only food item offered.
vm_kyoto_baisenya.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. The signature dish is vegan tsukemen (dipping noodles) served with a drink (drip coffee), and the menu contains no animal ingredients. The soup is built on a donko (dried shiitake) and kombu dashi with a soy-milk base, so the broth itself is plant-based with no fish dashi. Offered in limited quantities, reservation-only.

vm_saishoku_ryori_f_effe.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. They avoid regular wheat flour and instead use rice flour for breads and sweets, but their pasta is made from ancient-grain/spelt (spelled) flour, which DOES contain gluten and is unsafe for celiacs. Gluten-free pasta can be accommodated only when requested in advance with your reservation (multiple diners confirmed GF accommodation when booked a few days ahead). If you are gluten-free, you must notify the restaurant directly when reserving; no cross-contamination protocol is published, so ask about shared cookware. No published detail on whether soy sauce used is wheat-free.
vm_saishoku_ryori_f_effe.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. The couple who run it exclude all meat, fish, dairy, eggs and refined white sugar from every dish. Vegetables are sourced from trusted farmers. Note: they explicitly state they do NOT cater to "Eastern-style" veganism that excludes the five pungent spices (garlic/onion etc.), nor do they accommodate vegetable allergies, so confirm if you avoid alliums.

vm_vegan_ramen_uzu_kyoto.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The noodles in all ramen dishes are made from Hokkaido wheat flour and organic whole-grain flour from Aya, Miyazaki — so they contain wheat/gluten. The signature ramen also uses soy sauce; the source pages do not state whether a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used, so a celiac/gluten-sensitive diner should assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce and confirm directly. No dedicated gluten-free menu or GF noodle option was found.
vm_vegan_ramen_uzu_kyoto.vegan_detail: Entirely plant-based kitchen — every dish is vegan. The shared broth used across all ramen is a 12-hour infusion of Rausu kelp (kombu), shiitake mushrooms and vegetables, with no meat, fish or animal dashi. The flagship is the UZU Signature Course featuring a choice of Soy Sauce & Fermented Mugwort ramen or Spicy Miso & Sake Kasu ramen, plus vegan appetizers and dessert. Because the entire venue is vegan, there is no animal-product cross-contact in the kitchen.

vm_saisyoku_kousagisha.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The set plate leans heavily on soy-based and seasoned items (miso, soy milk, tofu, simmered/seasoned vegetables) where Japanese soy sauce and seasonings typically contain wheat, so gluten is very likely present and not separately controlled. There is no mention of wheat-free soy sauce, a dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocols. A celiac/GF diner should contact the restaurant directly in advance; the menu is largely Japanese-only, so communication may require Japanese.
vm_saisyoku_kousagisha.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. The single signature offering is a one-plate lunch (around 2,100-2,500 yen) of 10+ small seasonal dishes containing no meat, fish, eggs or dairy. Components reported include miso potage made with soy milk, homemade mugwort/koya (freeze-dried) tofu, simmered Oki seaweed, mountain vegetables and pickles, and a fresh-fruit dessert. Vegetables are pesticide-free/reduced-pesticide and sourced from trusted farmers, largely in northern Kyoto Prefecture. The owner is open about not being dogmatic ("enjoy delicious vegetables without becoming too strict"), but the dishes themselves are reported as entirely plant-based.

vm_alt_coffee_roasters.gf_detail: Multiple sources describe the cafe as offering gluten-free options, and several items (such as certain cakes and ice cream) are noted as gluten-free. However it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and not all items are GF (e.g. bagel sandwiches contain wheat). There is no published information on soy sauce wheat content, dedicated fryers, or flour cross-contamination, so a strictly gluten-free diner should confirm specific items with staff on the day. GF travelers are advised to ask which sweets are wheat-free.
vm_alt_coffee_roasters.vegan_detail: The Nijo Castle (Nakagyo) location is fully plant-based: the kitchen uses no milk, eggs or other dairy. Cow's milk was replaced with organic oat milk (Minor Figures) for lattes and other drinks, and the sweets and food are all vegan. Owner Chihiro Nakamura, a certified nutritionist, also avoids white sugar (using amazake / brown sugar / fermented seasonings). Signature items include a vegan "cheesecake" (brown sugar, homemade miso, fermented yuzu-lemon sauce, purple carrot), chocolate cake, seasonal-fruit cakes, and open bagel sandwiches with seasonal vegetables. Note: a separate sister branch in Yawata ("Iwashimizu") reportedly uses egg in an omelet-rice dish, but the Nijo location matching this listing is described as entirely vegan. Staff invite guests to mention vegan needs and allergies when ordering.

vm_soshoku_cafe_ren_shijo_o.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The kitchen leans heavily on soy/wheat-based mock meats ("vegan eel," fried soy-meat), tempura, curry bun and apple cake, all of which typically contain wheat. Brown rice and the herbal medicinal soup are served, which may be safer bases, but standard Japanese soy sauce (usually wheat-containing) is likely used in seasoning. A gluten-free diner should ask directly about each dish; assume wheat is present unless confirmed otherwise.
vm_soshoku_cafe_ren_shijo_o.vegan_detail: The entire menu is plant-based: the kitchen uses no meat, fish, eggs or dairy at all. It follows Taiwanese Buddhist "su si" (素食/精進料理) principles, which also exclude the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, leek, green onion, chives). This means the food is effectively vegan and additionally allium-free, which is helpful for diners avoiding onion/garlic. Signature plant-based dishes include "vegan eel" made from tofu, a daily vegetarian set meal with multiple mains and sides, yakuzen (medicinal) curry, and fried soy-meat with mugwort and lotus root. Because soy-meat (mock meat) and tempura/fried items are central to the menu, dishes are flavorful and clearly meat-free, but see GF notes for wheat caution.

vm_soshoku_cafe_ren_horikaw.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published by the cafe or on listing sites. The menu leans heavily on wheat-based items — dumplings, gyoza, radish cakes, glutinous-rice dumplings and noodles — and Taiwanese mock meats often contain wheat gluten (seitan), so cross-contact with wheat is likely. Soy sauce is presumably standard (wheat-containing) but not confirmed. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask directly about each dish; nothing is confirmed safe.
vm_soshoku_cafe_ren_horikaw.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based kitchen. The cafe states it uses no animal products whatsoever (no meat, fish, eggs or dairy) and follows Taiwanese Buddhist vegetarian (素食/精進) tradition. It also avoids the "five pungent vegetables" (onion, scallion, garlic, chives and related alliums), so dishes are free of onion and garlic. Mock-meat items (e.g. vegan "eel" rice bowl made from okara and seaweed, vegan takoyaki, dumplings, radish cakes) are made from plant ingredients. Everything on the menu is suitable for vegans.

vm_shokudo_yamagoya.gf_detail: Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners. The fixed menu leans heavily on wheat: signature items include wheat gluten ("fu") and wheat-gluten katsu, plus tempura and vegetable pakora (battered/fried), all of which contain wheat flour. Japanese soy sauce (almost certainly wheat-containing) is used in the cooking. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and the single-set format leaves little room to adapt for celiac/GF needs. No information was found confirming any GF accommodation.
vm_shokudo_yamagoya.vegan_detail: Effectively a fully vegan kitchen. There is a single fixed daily lunch set built around organic brown rice and local Uji vegetables, and reviewers confirm everything is vegan apart from one dessert (a cake) that is clearly labeled as containing egg and dairy. Savory dishes (tempeh, wheat-gluten "fu", tempura, vegetable pakora, miso soup, pickles) are plant-based. Because there is essentially one set menu, customization is very limited.

vm_gen.gf_detail: A fully gluten-free, wheat-free kitchen: no wheat flour is used anywhere. Their signature bread is made from 100% rice flour with no binders, and all sweets are gluten-free. This is a dedicated GF environment rather than a mixed kitchen with GF options, so cross-contamination from wheat flour is minimal. The brand of soy sauce/seasoning is not specified online, so a celiac-level diner should still confirm the soy sauce is a wheat-free (tamari-style) one, as standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat.
vm_gen.vegan_detail: Everything sold is 100% plant-based. The kitchen uses no animal products at all (no meat, fish, dairy, or eggs), and also avoids chemical seasonings and refined white sugar. Menu includes Ayurvedic-style spice curries, bento boxes, salad wraps, quiche, tofu karaage, soups, 100% rice-flour breads, and desserts; pre-order vegan nigiri sushi is also available. Because the whole operation is vegan, there is no animal-based cross-contact in the kitchen. Note: dashi and the specific soy sauce used are not documented online; confirm directly if you need to verify the seasoning is also free of animal-derived katsuo (fish) dashi.

vm_bishamondo_shourin_ji_sh.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Shojin ryori commonly includes wheat-based items (seasoned soy sauce, fu/wheat gluten, tempura batter, noodles) and the matcha is served with wagashi that often contain wheat or wheat-derived ingredients. Soy sauce used in shojin cooking is typically standard wheat-containing shoyu unless otherwise arranged. A gluten-free diner must ask in advance (call 075-561-4311 or use the reservation form); nothing here is confirmed GF-safe.
vm_bishamondo_shourin_ji_sh.vegan_detail: Shourin-ji is a Rinzai Zen temple (a sub-temple of Tofukuji). Food served to visitors follows shojin ryori (精進料理) tradition, which is plant-based by religious rule: no meat, fish, eggs, dairy or honey, and dashi (stock) is made from kombu (kelp) and dried shiitake rather than bonito. Two food offerings exist: (1) matcha green tea served with Japanese sweets after a zazen/sutra-copying session (a choice of dry sweets / higashi for about 800 yen or fresh sweets / namagashi for about 1000 yen added to the experience), and (2) a full shojin ryori meal experience available by advance reservation (must book by 16:00 two days before). Because it is a temple kitchen, the meal itself is reliably vegan; the accompanying wagashi sweets are traditionally vegan but the exact sweets vary, so confirm ingredients of the specific sweet on the day. No menu page independently itemizes every ingredient, so vegans should still state their requirement when booking.

vm_boulangerie_l_oiseau_ble.gf_detail: The bakery's core line is gluten-free, built on rice flour and sorghum (the "Life Bread" and many baked goods are explicitly gluten-free). However, the shop also bakes breads from ancient wheats (spelt/Kamut/einkorn), which DO contain gluten — these are marketed as "ancient wheat" rather than gluten-free. Gluten-free and ancient-wheat products are made in the same small kitchen, so a celiac diner should ask the owner which specific items are gluten-free and discuss cross-contact. No dedicated gluten-free-only facility is confirmed.
vm_boulangerie_l_oiseau_ble.vegan_detail: 100% vegan bakery — all products are free of eggs, dairy and honey, with no meat, fish or any animal ingredients (Vege Project / VegeMap certified as all-vegan). Breads are made from organic, pesticide-free rice flour, sorghum (takakibi), and ancient wheats. The signature "Life Bread" (命のパン) is a slow-fermented enzyme bread.

vm_itadakizen_kyoto_itadaki.gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen in Kyoto (the brand's London branch is fully gluten-free, but Kyoto is not). Some dishes can be made gluten-free — the tofu steak can be prepared GF and there is reportedly a GF option for some items — but fried items share oil, so they are not celiac-safe. Soy sauce/wheat content of seasonings is not confirmed; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so a strict GF or celiac diner should explicitly inform staff and ask whether wheat-free tamari is used. Staff are described as accommodating with allergies — notify them when booking and confirm directly.
vm_itadakizen_kyoto_itadaki.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen — no animal products, dairy, eggs or honey. The menu is entirely plant-based, built around fermented soy (house-made miso), soy milk, tofu and seasonal Kyoto vegetables. Signature plant dishes include vegan sushi (a dinner-only specialty topped with fresh Kyoto vegetables), vegetable-and-soy-milk vegan ramen, grilled onigiri, tofu steak, enmen soy-milk noodles, and Korean-style bibimbap and kimbap. Vegetables are organic/pesticide-free and locally sourced. Because the entire kitchen is animal-free, dashi is plant-based (no fish stock).

vm_gomacro_salon.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free kitchen. Many savory dishes (sesame dandan noodles, curry noodles/ramen, pasta) are wheat-based, and at least one gluten-free diner reported being unable to eat any of the savory options and settling for a nut pie dessert. There is no documented confirmation that the soy sauce is wheat-free or that a dedicated fryer is used. A celiac/GF traveler should ask item by item; safe choices are limited.
vm_gomacro_salon.vegan_detail: The café kitchen is plant-based: dishes are made without eggs, dairy, or other animal ingredients. The one honest caveat is that a few dressings/items contain honey, and these are clearly marked on the menu, so strict vegans should ask or watch for the honey labels. Sesame is central to nearly every dish (it is the antenna café of sesame-oil maker Yamada Seiyu), so sesame-allergic diners should avoid. Dashi-based soups use sesame miso rather than fish dashi.

vm_natural_food_village.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised, and several signature items contain or likely contain wheat: soy meat / soy "cutlet," tempeh tempura (batter), Thai fried noodles, spring rolls, and curry. Soy meat and seitan-style proteins are wheat-based, and there is no confirmation that a wheat-free (tamari) soy sauce is used. Gluten-sensitive diners should ask directly about each dish; assume standard wheat-containing soy sauce and batters unless confirmed otherwise.
vm_natural_food_village.vegan_detail: Fully vegan (plant-based) kitchen — described as one of the oldest all-vegan restaurants in Kyoto (est. 2001). The entire menu is plant-based, built around organic, pesticide-free vegetables, ancient grains, and house seasonings, with groundwater drawn from Kitashirakawa. Signature dishes are the Rainbow Bento (虹色御膳, ~¥1,200, seven deli sides + ancient rice + soup) and soy-cutlet/soy-meat rice bowls. No animal products are used, so dairy/egg/fish cross-contact is inherently minimal.

vm_yoshifumi.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The macrobiotic/grain-based menu leans heavily on rice (enzyme rice, seven-grain rice) and miso/soy-based seasoning, but wheat-containing items (soy sauce, possibly dumpling wrappers, seven-grain blends) are likely present. Whether the soy sauce or miso is wheat-free, and whether dishes can be made gluten-free, is unconfirmed and must be asked directly in advance.
vm_yoshifumi.vegan_detail: Listed by Japan VegeMap as an all-vegan eatery (飲食店（全てヴィーガン）), meaning no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy, or honey. Described as a 穀物菜食 (grain-based/macrobiotic) home cafe serving seasonal vegetable set meals. A reported lunch set includes vegetable dumplings in a soymilk soup, fried daikon, pickles, and a choice of enzyme rice or seven-grain rice, with dessert and tea. Everything served is plant-based.

vm_cacao_magic.gf_detail: No explicit gluten-free information is published. Raw chocolate bars themselves are cacao/nut/superfood based and many are naturally grain-free, but the signature raw chocolate tart has a crust whose ingredients are not documented as gluten-free. Because soy sauce/dashi are not part of this dessert-focused menu, the main gluten concern is tart crusts and baked items. A coeliac should ask per item; no dedicated GF claim exists.
vm_cacao_magic.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based raw chocolate specialty shop. All chocolate and sweets are organic, dairy-free, egg-free and made with no animal products whatsoever ("動物を一切使っていない"), no white sugar, no additives or preservatives. Raw process keeps everything below 48°C. Cafe items (raw chocolate tart, cacao smoothie, iced raw chocolate, ice cream) use plant milks such as almond milk. Suitable for vegans without modification.

vm_izusen_daijiin_ten.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made. Shojin cuisine relies heavily on wheat-based ingredients: seitan/wheat-gluten (fu, namafu), tempura/fried items, and Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat). Set courses are not adjustable a la carte. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should ask in advance and treat soy sauce, fu, and fried elements as containing wheat unless individually confirmed.
vm_izusen_daijiin_ten.vegan_detail: This is traditional shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian cuisine) served within the grounds of Daitoku-ji temple. By definition shojin uses no meat, fish, or seafood, and the kitchen also avoids the "five pungent" aromatics (onion, garlic, etc.). The official site explicitly states no meat, fish, or seafood are used and that dashi is plant-based rather than animal/fish stock — making the food effectively vegan/vegetarian throughout. Diners with strict vegan needs should note shojin traditionally permits ingredients like dairy/honey rarely, but the cuisine here is plant-centered; confirm any specific concern when reserving.

vm_kyono_juice.gf_detail: No source states a gluten-free policy. The core product — cold-pressed juices from raw fruit and vegetables — is inherently free of wheat/gluten, and smoothies and cold-pressed sodas are likewise plant/fruit based. However, there is no published allergen information; VegeMap explicitly says to confirm allergens directly with the shop. A celiac diner should ask about any cereal/grain powders, granola toppings, or syrups before ordering. Treat as likely-safe-but-unconfirmed.
vm_kyono_juice.vegan_detail: A cold-pressed juice and smoothie specialty stand whose menu is built entirely on fresh fruit and vegetables, cold-pressed sodas and smoothies — listed as fully vegan (すべてヴィーガン) on Japan VegeMap. No dairy or animal products are mentioned in any source describing the menu. Note that smoothies and any blended items may contain plant milk or other add-ins, so a strict vegan should confirm specific blends in person. The shop is the antenna store of Sun Chlorella's subsidiary, so chlorella/spirulina superfood additions appear in some drinks (all plant-based).

vm_izusen_murasakino_shojin.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Signature dishes rely on wheat-derived ingredients — yuba (the house specialty), fu (wheat gluten) and ordinary wheat-based soy sauce are central to the cuisine and cannot be removed. No GF accommodation is indicated. Celiac/GF diners are not safe here.
vm_izusen_murasakino_shojin.vegan_detail: Shojin ryori is fully plant-based by Buddhist precept — no meat or seafood. The official site states dishes use only grains, legumes and vegetables, and Tabelog states animal-derived broths are avoided, so meals are intended to be vegetarian/vegan. However, this is traditional shojin (not a self-described "vegan" kitchen): a HappyCow reviewer historically reported a bonito/fish flavour in the dashi and said staff once confirmed fish was used for broth (later reviewers reported no fish). Yuba, tofu and seasonal vegetables are the mainstays. Vegans should confirm at booking that no katsuo/fish dashi is used in their course.

vm_cafe_millet.gf_detail: No gluten-free guarantee is published. The set lunch is bread- and wheat-forward (stone-oven house bread, vegan pizza, quiche), so gluten is present in the standard course. There is no documented dedicated gluten-free menu or stated wheat-free soy sauce. Because it is reservation-only and the kitchen tailors the course, gluten-free or wheat-restricted diners should phone or email ahead (075-888-0878 / millet@cafemillet.jp) to ask what can be adapted; nothing specific is confirmed.
vm_cafe_millet.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen. Cafe Millet serves a single multi-course organic vegan lunch built around vegetables grown on their own pesticide-free farm in Shizuhara. No animal products are used in the food (one optional addition is local honey served with the house-baked bread, which strict vegans may decline). Typical courses include grilled seasonal vegetables, tofu, vegan quiche, squash/vegetable soup, vegan pizza, salad, brown rice with aduki beans, stone-oven bread, and an apple & sweet potato tart, plus herb tea and coffee. The map listing notes it is an all-vegan establishment; staff recommend telling them about vegan needs and allergies when you reserve so the menu can be tailored.

vm_kanga_an.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is made. Fucha ryori relies heavily on deep-fried dishes (a defining feature is its skillful use of oil) and on tofu/seitan-style preparations, plus standard Japanese soy sauce, mirin and sweetened glazes, all of which typically contain wheat. Tempura batter and the imitation-meat/eel dishes are likely wheat-based. A gluten-free diner would need to discuss the menu in advance; gluten avoidance cannot be assumed safe here.
vm_kanga_an.vegan_detail: Kanga-an serves fucha ryori (普茶料理), the Obaku Zen school's form of shojin (Buddhist vegetarian) cuisine, which by tradition contains no meat, fish, or animal products. Courses are multi-dish (10+ items) and famously include plant-based imitation dishes such as "vegan unagi (eel)" made from tofu, seaweed, and burdock. Shojin/fucha cooking traditionally uses kombu (kelp) and shiitake-based dashi rather than fish/bonito dashi, so it is generally plant-based throughout. That said, the kitchen does not publish an explicit allergen/vegan certification, so strict vegans should confirm when reserving that no honey, dairy, or egg is used in any specific course.

vm_little_heaven.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free-friendly spot. The kitchen uses fresh namafu (生麩, a wheat-gluten product) and serves house-made natural-yeast bread, so wheat/gluten is central to the menu. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is advertised, and the owner states they normally do not accommodate food allergies. Soy sauce is used but there is no confirmation it is wheat-free (standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat) — gluten-sensitive diners should treat this as unsafe unless they confirm directly. A celiac/GF traveler should ask before booking and assume gluten is present.
vm_little_heaven.vegan_detail: Fully vegan kitchen: 100% plant-based with no dairy, eggs, fish, meat, or honey used anywhere. Dishes are built around seasonal Kyoto vegetables plus fresh yuba (tofu skin) and namafu (fresh wheat gluten). Everything served is vegan, so plant-based diners can order any item on the (reservation-only) course without modification.

vm_tenryuji_shigetsu.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is advertised. The cuisine is plant-based and many components (rice, simmered vegetables, sesame tofu, pickles) are naturally wheat-free, but standard Japanese soy sauce and miso glazes typically contain wheat, and some dishes use wheat gluten (fu) or tempura-style preparations common in shojin ryori. A celiac/strict-GF diner should ask in advance — the temple takes reservations by phone (3 days ahead for the larger courses) where dietary requests can be raised.
vm_tenryuji_shigetsu.vegan_detail: Shojin ryori prepared by Tenryu-ji temple's own kitchen using no animal-based ingredients whatsoever ("動物性の素材を一切使用せず") — built on vegetables, mountain vegetables (sansai), wild plants, and seaweed. Traditional shojin cuisine also excludes the five pungent vegetables (garlic, onion, etc.). Dashi is plant-based (kombu seaweed) rather than fish-based, so the kitchen is genuinely vegan, not merely vegetarian. Signature dishes include goma-dofu (sesame tofu), yuba (tofu skin) in clear broth, miso-glazed eggplant, and seasonal simmered/pickled vegetables. The full course is a one-soup, five/six/seven-side-dish set depending on the tier.

vm_hakuunan.gf_detail: Not gluten-free by default. Fucha ryori centers on wheat-heavy preparations: seasoned tempura (wheat-flour battered and fried), mock-meat "modoki" dishes that often use wheat gluten (fu/seitan), and Japanese soy sauce that typically contains wheat. There is no published gluten-free menu and no information about a dedicated fryer. A celiac/gluten-sensitive diner would need to discuss this directly when reserving (reservation required by 5pm the previous day), and even then safe options would likely be very limited. No GF accommodation is confirmed.
vm_hakuunan.vegan_detail: Hakuunan serves traditional Obaku fucha ryori (普茶料理), a Chinese-derived form of Buddhist shojin cuisine that is plant-based by tradition: no meat, fish, eggs or dairy. The standard course is built on "two soups and six dishes" (二汁六菜) and includes sesame tofu (goma-dofu), yoshino-ni stew, seasonal vegetables and seasoned tempura. Classic shojin/fucha cooking generally avoids animal products, but some traditional kitchens use bonito (katsuo) dashi unless kombu-only stock is requested, so vegans should state their needs when reserving. The restaurant's listing advises telling staff about vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies so the menu can be adjusted, and both vegan and vegetarian course options are noted as available. Treat it as plant-based-by-default but confirm dashi and any honey at booking.

vm_to_rin_in.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Shojin ryori relies heavily on regular Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) for simmered dishes and dressings, and may include wheat gluten preparations (fu) and tofu products marinated in soy sauce. There is no indication of wheat-free soy sauce or a dedicated GF kitchen. A gluten-sensitive or celiac diner must inquire directly in advance; assume soy sauce contains wheat unless confirmed otherwise.
vm_to_rin_in.vegan_detail: Tōrin-in serves traditional shojin ryori (Buddhist temple cuisine), which by religious practice excludes all meat, fish, and animal products — it is plant-based throughout. The abbot Nishikawa Genbō, a published shojin cookbook author, prepares the meals. Documented dishes include rice, mushroom-and-mitsuba clear soup, chrysanthemum-greens shiraae (tofu dressing), simmered carrot and burdock, furofuki daikon, and pickles; seasonal events such as the Azuki-gayu (red-bean rice porridge) gathering serve azuki porridge, simmered black beans, daikon with abura-age, rape-blossom shiraae, and salted kelp. Shojin ryori traditionally uses kombu (kelp) and shiitake dashi rather than fish (bonito) dashi, so the broth is plant-based — but specific ingredient confirmation is not published, so a strict vegan should still confirm on the day.

vm_apelila.gf_detail: This is a wheat-forward bakery. The signature products are breads and baked goods made from house-milled Kyoto whole-wheat flour, so gluten is central to nearly everything. No gluten-free menu, certification, or dedicated preparation is mentioned by any source. Not suitable for celiacs or those avoiding gluten.
vm_apelila.vegan_detail: 100% plant-based (vegan) bakery and café. All breads and baked goods use no butter, eggs, or dairy. Bread is made from house-milled Kyoto whole-wheat flour and a natural sourdough starter kept going for ~20 years. The café serves lunch including a vegan cheeseburger plate (Kyoto whole-wheat bun, chickpea patty, fermented soy yogurt), plus vegan donuts, melon bread, caramel cream pastries, cookies and waffles. Soy-based cream and plant milks are used in place of dairy. No animal products are used anywhere in the kitchen.

vm_mamezen_tonyu_ramen_senm.gf_detail: Not a dedicated GF kitchen, but genuinely GF-aware: gluten-free rice noodles are available as a swap (+250 yen), and the official menu states a wheat-free soy sauce is used when the rice noodles are selected. Standard ramen noodles contain wheat. The kitchen uses a single boiling pot but separate preparation can be requested, so a celiac should explicitly ask for the rice-noodle/wheat-free-soy preparation and confirm separate cooking.
vm_mamezen_tonyu_ramen_senm.vegan_detail: Everything served is 100% vegan, no animal products at all. Since 2014 the kitchen follows an "Oriental Vegan" approach rooted in shojin (Buddhist temple) cuisine, excluding eggs, fish stock, the five pungent alliums (no green onion/garlic/onion), and using no fish dashi. Signature dishes are the Musashi soy-milk ramen (yuba/tofu skin, mizuna, pickled plum, yuzu, sansho mushrooms, seaweed) and a soybean-meat tantan ramen.

vm_kissa_hobodo_cafe_hobodo.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is made by the cafe and none of the sources document GF accommodation. The menu includes clearly wheat-containing items (homemade flatbread, spring rolls/gyoza wrappers, falafel, sweets) and Japanese set meals typically use standard miso and soy sauce, which usually contain wheat. Brown-rice and rice-based dishes (e.g. edamame rice, some ohagi) may be naturally gluten-free, but no dedicated GF preparation or wheat-free soy sauce is confirmed. A gluten-sensitive diner should ask in person about each dish; the staff speak some English.
vm_kissa_hobodo_cafe_hobodo.vegan_detail: A fully plant-based (vegan) cafe and bookshop. Multiple sources confirm everything on the menu is vegan ("全てヴィーガンOK") - no meat, fish, dairy, eggs or honey. The owner's wife, who runs the kitchen, follows a plant-based lifestyle and the cafe was founded so "everyone can eat together without worry." Food centers on seasonal vegetables sourced from local organic/natural farms: a daily set meal (brown or edamame rice, miso soup, and several vegetable side dishes), plus dishes like vegetable-and-bean curry, pumpkin-carrot soup, spring rolls/gyoza, falafel, and six varieties of brown-rice ohagi (sweet rice cakes). Because the kitchen is entirely vegan, there is no animal-protein cross-contact.

vm_seigen_in_saigen_in.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The core dish is tofu, vegetables and the seven herbs of yudofu, which can be naturally GF, but the dipping sauce and any soy sauce / seasonings are an unknown gluten risk (typical Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, and the dipping sauce is reportedly fish-based and otherwise unspecified). The shojin set may include other components. A gluten-sensitive diner must ask directly about the sauce, soy sauce and condiments; staff speak only limited English, so a written allergy card in Japanese is advisable.
vm_seigen_in_saigen_in.vegan_detail: Seigen-in serves a seven-herb yudofu (hot tofu pot, ~2,000 yen) and a shojin ryori (Buddhist vegetarian) set with tofu pot (~4,000 yen). The food itself is plant-based tofu-and-vegetable temple fare, so the bulk of the meal is suitable for vegetarians and largely for vegans. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: multiple HappyCow reviewers report that the dipping sauce served with the yudofu is fish-based (not vegan) — so despite the VegeMap "all-vegan" tag, a strict vegan should explicitly decline the dipping sauce and ask what condiments contain. As a temple-style (shojin) kitchen it should avoid meat, but vegans cannot assume the dashi/sauce is plant-based and must ask. Best treated as vegetarian-friendly with vegan possible on request.

vm_tenki_sora_tenki_oozora.gf_detail: Some gluten-free items are available, notably gluten-free sweets (described as made by a young 17-year-old chef) and rice-based omusubi/inari. The shop uses a carefully fermented, additive-free soy sauce, but it is not confirmed to be wheat-free (most Japanese soy sauce contains wheat), so celiac diners should ask before ordering anything seasoned. No information confirms a dedicated gluten-free prep area, so this is GF-options rather than dedicated GF.
vm_tenki_sora_tenki_oozora.vegan_detail: A small organic takeout shop, not fully plant-based: free-range eggs are used in the kitchen, so it is best described as having vegan options rather than being vegan. Staff explicitly cater to vegans and the shop is listed as offering vegan, vegetarian and macrobiotic choices. Vegan-friendly items include plant-based fermented brown-rice omusubi, organic inari sushi, the "additive-free welcome soup" made with plant-based natural ingredients, and smoothies. Because the menu of 6-7 daily side dishes changes every day and eggs are present in the kitchen, vegans should confirm which specific items are plant-based on the day of their visit.

vm_kamogawa_bakery_kyoto_ho.gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery — breads and bagels are made from domestic (Hokkaido) wheat flour, so products are not gluten-free and the environment is heavily flour-dusted. No gluten-free items are advertised. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
vm_kamogawa_bakery_kyoto_ho.vegan_detail: Roughly 80% of the lineup is plant-based: the kitchen does not use butter, milk or eggs in its core breads, instead using soy-milk butter and confirming its beet sugar is animal-free. Most loaves (soy-milk butter "new white bread"/shokupan made with Hokkaido Haruyokoi wheat) and many bagels are vegan, and there is an egg-allergy-friendly "hobo-tama" sandwich. However it is NOT a fully vegan shop: some bagel sandwiches contain salmon, shrimp/avocado and cream cheese. Vegans should select the plant-based loaves/bagels and the plant-based burger and confirm fillings, since animal-based sandwich options share the counter.

vm_ikuri_cafe.gf_detail: Some naturally gluten-free items but not a GF-safe kitchen. The 100% buckwheat (Dattan) soba is naturally gluten-free, and a wheat-free plant-based soy sauce option is offered with the noodles. However, the brown rice udon contains wheat (roughly 30% whole brown-rice flour mixed with wheat flour), and there is no stated dedicated gluten-free preparation protocol or separate cooking water. A celiac diner should confirm cross-contact directly; soba and udon are likely boiled in shared water.
vm_ikuri_cafe.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based whole-foods café — the entire menu is vegan. Dishes are built from unrefined, unprocessed ingredients with no animal protein, fat, or excess sugar/oil/salt. Offerings include an energy plant-based whole-food salad set, a soy-meat burger, brown rice udon, 100% buckwheat (Dattan) soba, brown-rice rice balls, daily vegetable soups, raw cakes/tarts (e.g. raw matcha tiramisu), sourdough toast, and soy/oat-milk beverages and smoothies. Broths use kombu (kelp) dashi only — no fish dashi. The kitchen also accommodates 五葷抜き (no five pungent alliums) on request.

vm_tantanmen_yebisu_ebisu_e.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is wheat ramen noodles, and tantanmen seasonings typically include wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free noodles, dedicated equipment, or wheat-free soy sauce indicated. A celiac or strict GF diner is not accommodated here.
vm_tantanmen_yebisu_ebisu_e.vegan_detail: Primarily a meat-based tantanmen restaurant, but it offers one dedicated vegan dish: the Vegetable Tantanmen (たっぷり野菜の担担麺), in which Kyoto tofu replaces the usual minced pork and the broth is made with no animal ingredients (per the VegeMap listing for this branch). Plenty of vegetables top it. Noodles are standard wheat ramen noodles. Most other items contain meat/dairy, so the vegan traveler should specifically order the vegetable/tofu tantanmen and confirm no animal products (動物性なし) when ordering.

vm_oc_burrito_formerly_la_s.gf_detail: Corn tortillas are available as an alternative to flour/wheat tortillas, and the Vegan Taco BOX is built on corn tortillas, so naturally gluten-free components (corn tortilla, rice, black beans, salsa, guacamole, chips) exist. However this is a casual shared-prep counter with flour tortillas, cheese and house salsas on the same line, and no dedicated gluten-free or fryer protocol is documented. Whether the chips are fried in a shared fryer and whether the salsa/lime cream contain wheat is not confirmed. A celiac diner should ask staff directly and request corn tortillas.
vm_oc_burrito_formerly_la_s.vegan_detail: An omnivore Chipotle-style counter restaurant with clearly labeled plant-based items: a Vegan Taco BOX (3 corn tortillas, green rice/Arroz Verde, black beans, house salsa and chips) and a Vegan Burrito with chips and salsa. Note the separate "vegetarian" quesadilla/nachos/burrito are NOT vegan, since the standard build includes cheese and lime (sour) cream and flour tortillas; order specifically from the vegan items or ask for cheese and lime cream to be omitted. Beans, rice, guacamole and fresh salsa (tomato, lettuce, cilantro, pickles) are the plant-based core. Soy/tofu meat substitute is not documented; the plant filling is vegetables and beans rather than mock meat.

vm_ron_herman_cafe_kyoto.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or GF labeling was found on the official site, the cafe menu pages, or listing directories. The kitchen cooks to order and welcomes allergy requests, so some dishes may be adaptable, but cross-contact with wheat (baked goods, cakes, sauces) is likely in a general cafe kitchen. A gluten-free diner should ask staff directly about each dish and seasonings.
vm_ron_herman_cafe_kyoto.vegan_detail: An omnivore California-style cafe that offers a small set of clearly labeled vegan dishes made without any animal-derived ingredients. Documented vegan items include a vegan rice bowl (black rice with 8 kinds of vegetables, around 1,350 yen), a vegan avocado cake (around 550 yen), and oat-milk banana juice made with plant-based milk (around 900 yen, with toppings like matcha, cacao nibs, mint). The cafe says it plans to expand its vegan lineup. Specific details on whether the vegan dishes use a wheat-free soy sauce or any dashi were not published; ask staff to confirm seasonings. Because the cafe cooks to order and explicitly invites guests to flag allergies and disliked ingredients, customization is realistic.

vm_pettirosso_kyoto.gf_detail: Gluten-free sweets are explicitly offered, and one reviewer described "tons of gluten-free options." However there is no published detail on whether the soy sauce / seasonings are wheat-free, no dedicated GF prep area is mentioned, and no celiac cross-contamination protocol is documented. A celiac diner should call or message ahead (English is fine) and confirm specific dishes and soy-sauce wheat content.
vm_pettirosso_kyoto.vegan_detail: Since relocating to Kita-ku in May 2023, the kitchen is entirely plant-based — all dishes contain no animal products (the earlier Shimogyo location had served local fish, but that is no longer the case). The lunch is a one-plate vegan meal with four deli-style sides, salad and a choice of main; dinner is a multi-course vegan set. More than 30 kinds of organic vegetables (from Ohara, Hanase and other Kyoto areas) are used, with nuts for richness, fermented foods for depth and spices as accents. Vegan cakes/sweets made without eggs, dairy or refined sugar are available.

vm_hon_to_yasai_oyoy_book_a.gf_detail: Listings indicate a gluten-free menu / gluten-free options are available, but no detail is published on dedicated preparation, cross-contamination control, or whether the soy sauce used is wheat-free. A gluten-sensitive diner should ask directly about each dish, since carrot cake and other baked desserts likely contain wheat.
vm_hon_to_yasai_oyoy_book_a.vegan_detail: The kitchen is fully vegetarian (no meat or fish on the menu). The signature OyOy Seasonal Vegetable Plate (about 1,850 yen) is made entirely from plant-based ingredients and is explicitly recommended for vegans; this full plate is served Fri-Mon (Monday lunch only). On other weekdays only lighter snacks, desserts and drinks are available, and some of those items (and some desserts) are vegetarian-but-not-vegan, containing dairy or egg (e.g. egg custard). Vegans should aim for Fri-Sun (or Mon lunch) for the full vegan plate and confirm individual desserts. Specific soy sauce wheat content and dashi sourcing are not documented publicly; the cuisine is vegetable-forward and uses soy products like tofu.

vm_social_kitchen.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The café serves cakes, cookies, curries and lunch plates, and standard Japanese soy sauce (which normally contains wheat) is the regional default, so wheat/gluten should be assumed present unless you ask. Because the kitchen rotates among different operators by day, any gluten-free accommodation would need to be confirmed directly with whoever is cooking that day. Treat as "ask" — no dedicated GF handling confirmed.
vm_social_kitchen.vegan_detail: Social Kitchen's 1F café is a community-minded eatery centered on locally grown ingredients and low-waste cooking, and it offers plant-based dishes (vegan items such as a South Indian bean curry have appeared on its menu). Important: the café now runs as a "share café," with different cooks/operators taking different days of the week, so vegan availability and the exact menu vary day to day. If you want a guaranteed vegan meal, check that day's operator in advance. Not a fully vegan kitchen — animal-based dishes are also served depending on the day.

vm_kyodai_shokudo_cafeteria.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. As a standard Japanese cafeteria, soy sauce and seasonings almost certainly contain wheat, and there is no indication of GF-dedicated prep or wheat-free options. A celiac/GF diner should ask staff directly and not assume the certified-vegan items are also gluten-free.
vm_kyodai_shokudo_cafeteria.vegan_detail: An omnivore university cafeteria (open to the public, not just students) that offers a dedicated lineup of plant-based dishes meeting VegeProject Japan's vegan certification standard (no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs, or honey). It also runs a halal counter with items like a soy-fried tofu wrap. Vegan dishes are clearly marked, but they share a busy cafeteria line with omnivore food, so vegan offerings are a subset of the menu rather than the whole kitchen.

vm_mimikou_kyo_no_curry_udo.gf_detail: This is fundamentally a wheat-udon shop, so the signature udon and ramen dishes are NOT gluten-free (udon noodles are wheat). Some directory listings tag a "gluten-free menu," but the only plausibly gluten-free item is the curry rice with vegetables, and even then the curry sauce and the regular dashi are seasoned with soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and the shared frying oil handles battered/wheat items. There is no evidence of a dedicated gluten-free setup or wheat-free soy sauce. A celiac diner should ask directly and treat the noodle dishes as off-limits.
vm_mimikou_kyo_no_curry_udo.vegan_detail: Mimikou is an omnivore curry-udon shop (it also serves meat and fish), but it keeps a separate English vegetarian/vegan menu. The vegetarian udon broth is made without fish stock, using a kombu/seaweed (kelp) base instead of the usual katsuobushi (bonito) dashi found in their regular curry udon. Vegan choices include vegetarian/vegan curry udon, vegan dashi ramen, and curry rice with vegetables, customizable with vegetable tempura and deep-fried soy meat ("soy nuggets"). On the vegan menu, egg is an optional add-on, so order without it. Note one important caveat reported by a 2025 diner: staff said all frying (tofu, meat, fish) is done in the same oil, so fried toppings such as tempura and soy meat are not free of animal cross-contact. Confirm "no egg" and ask about the frying oil when ordering.

vm_salon_fu.gf_detail: No gluten-free information could be confirmed for this venue. Macrobiotic cooking in Japan typically relies on wheat-based ingredients such as standard soy sauce (shoyu), miso, seitan and wheat gluten (fu), so gluten exposure is likely. A celiac/GF diner must ask directly; whether tamari (wheat-free soy sauce) is used is unknown.
vm_salon_fu.vegan_detail: Listed on the curated Japan VegeMap directory as a macrobiotic cafe offering vegan options (not a fully vegan kitchen). Macrobiotic cooking generally avoids meat, eggs, dairy and refined sugar, so plant-based choices are likely available, but the menu is not confirmed to be entirely vegan. The listing itself is marked "not verified," so diners should tell staff about vegan needs and confirm individual dishes (especially regarding dashi, bonito, and any animal-derived seasonings) before ordering. No specific vegan dish list could be confirmed from public sources.

vm_kyodai_shokudo_hokubu_sh.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. This is a high-volume cafeteria serving fried foods, noodles (udon/ramen) and breaded items alongside vegan dishes, so cross-contact with wheat is likely. Menu cards do display allergen information (wheat is one of Japan's mandatory labeled allergens), so a diner can check individual items, but GF travelers should ask staff directly. Japanese soy sauce and standard dashi-based seasonings commonly used in cafeteria cooking typically contain wheat; assume soy sauce is NOT wheat-free unless confirmed.
vm_kyodai_shokudo_hokubu_sh.vegan_detail: This is the North Cafeteria (Hokubu Shokudo) of Kyoto University's co-op, an omnivore student/staff canteen that is open to the public.
