It carries a certified vegan menu as part of the VegeProject initiative that began at Kyoto University in 2013. VegeMap lists it as having certified vegan options, where "vegan" is defined as containing no meat, seafood, eggs, dairy or honey. Vegan dishes are a small dedicated portion of an otherwise meat- and fish-heavy cafeteria menu, so it is a cafeteria-style pick-your-tray operation rather than a vegan restaurant. Vegan items are labeled, and menu cards display allergen information. Staff recommend telling them about your vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies before choosing.

vm_browny_bread_bagels.gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery built around bagels, bread and focaccia made from domestic wheat with long fermentation. No gluten-free products, separate prep, or GF accommodations were found in any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners.
vm_browny_bread_bagels.vegan_detail: A wheat bakery offering 20-30 bagel varieties daily plus white bread and focaccia. Many of the plainer and savory items (made with domestic wheat, water, oil) are vegan-friendly, and soy milk is available. However, a large share of the lineup is explicitly non-vegan: cheese, cream cheese, gorgonzola-honey, tiramisu, bacon-cheese and similar varieties. There is no dedicated vegan menu or in-shop vegan labeling found, so plant-based diners should ask staff which specific bagels are egg-free and dairy-free. Confirmed as "has vegan options" rather than fully vegan.

vm_gohan_pan_kobo_tsubutsub.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The rice bread is cooked rice kneaded together with wheat flour (Hokkaido wheat), so all breads contain wheat/gluten.
vm_gohan_pan_kobo_tsubutsub.vegan_detail: Rice-bread bakery whose dough kneads freshly cooked Kyoto-blend rice into wheat flour. The base bread is plant-based, using soy milk and rice oil instead of eggs and dairy, and many items contain no eggs, dairy, or honey. Items with animal products are clearly labeled, so vegan diners can select safe options. Not all-vegan, as some products contain eggs, dairy, and honey.

vm_cafe_frosch.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or options are documented. The café's identity centers on house-baked bread made with whole-grain wheat flour and natural koji yeast, so wheat/gluten is pervasive in the kitchen. There is no information confirming wheat-free soy sauce or any gluten-free preparation. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should treat this as not gluten-safe and ask directly before ordering.
vm_cafe_frosch.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore café (it serves a German sausage sandwich and dishes that may contain dairy/eggs), but it offers a clear set of dedicated vegan options. Reported vegan items include a vegan aubergine (eggplant) burger set served with soup, side, salad and pickles; koji-bread plates with a vegan soup option; an onigiri rice-ball vegan set; and a "little bread" set with vegan soup and salad. The house bread is risen German-sourdough style using koji (a plant-based fermentation culture), and is described as entirely plant-based. Some vegetarian dishes contain dairy or eggs but can often be modified by removing animal products. Staff explicitly recommend telling them your vegetarian/vegan needs and any allergies so they can guide your order.

vm_omen_nippon_shijo_pontoc.gf_detail: This is a wheat-udon restaurant — the signature noodles are made from Japanese wheat flour and are not gluten-free. The dipping sauce/tare and any soy sauce used are conventional (wheat-containing); no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free noodle option was found. Tempura batter also contains wheat. Celiac/strict GF travelers cannot eat the core dishes here.
vm_omen_nippon_shijo_pontoc.vegan_detail: Omen is an omnivore udon house, but it maintains a dedicated vegan/vegetarian menu available on request. The plant-based set comes with a vegan dashi (fish-free, kelp/vegetable-based dipping broth) instead of the standard bonito dashi, an assortment of fresh and pickled seasonal Kyoto vegetables, ground sesame, and egg-free vegetable tempura. Omen was noted as one of the first noodle shops to explicitly offer a fish-free (vegetarian) dashi. You must ask for the vegan/vegetarian version when ordering, since the default menu is not plant-based.

vm_indo_shokudo_tadka_tadka.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. South Indian cooking here is largely rice- and lentil-based (rice meals, dosa, sambar, rasam, chutney), which is naturally wheat-free, but breads such as roti/parotta contain wheat and asafoetida (hing) used in many dishes is often wheat-cut. There is no published information on dedicated GF prep, soy-sauce contents, or dashi. A celiac/GF traveler must ask directly about each dish.
vm_indo_shokudo_tadka_tadka.vegan_detail: The standard/default lunch menu at TADKA1 is vegetarian (South Indian "Meals"), and the kitchen will prepare vegan versions on request ("定番メニューがベジタリアンで、ヴィーガン対応もしてもらえます"). The VEG MEALS set (around 9 dishes made "with beans and vegetables only") and a SIMPLE VEG SET are vegetable/legume based. Because South Indian vegetarian cooking commonly uses ghee, yogurt, paneer and dairy, vegan diners should explicitly ask for dairy-free preparation. No published per-dish ghee/dairy breakdown was found, so confirm in person or via Instagram (@indosyokudo_tadka).

vm_yuba_ryori_higashiyama_y.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are published. Soy sauce (very likely standard wheat-containing) appears in dishes such as the egg-yolk soy sauce yuba rice, and tempura/fried items and seasonings may contain wheat. Yuba and tofu-milk dishes themselves are naturally wheat-free, but cross-use of regular soy sauce and the lack of any stated gluten protocol means a celiac/GF diner must ask directly and cannot assume safety.
vm_yuba_ryori_higashiyama_y.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore restaurant built around house-made yuba (tofu skin), which is itself plant-based. Many signature elements are vegetable- and soy-forward: the "tofu milk hot pot" with Kyoto vegetables, fresh raw yuba, and yuba-based dishes. However, the kaiseki courses are NOT vegan — they incorporate Miyama-raised Kyoto jidori chicken, free-range egg (e.g. yuba rice topped with egg-yolk soy sauce), and very likely fish-based dashi in soups and seasonings. There is no published dedicated vegan or vegetarian course. A vegan or vegetarian diner would need to phone ahead (075-532-1019) and request a plant-only arrangement; the restaurant explicitly asks for advance notice of allergies and dietary needs, so accommodation may be possible but is not guaranteed. Treat as "ask."

vm_gojo_paradiso.gf_detail: Tabelog lists a gluten-free menu as available, so some GF choices exist, but no detail was found on dedicated preparation, separate fryers, or whether seasonings are wheat-free. Pita and naan are wheat-based, so order falafel/hummus plates without bread. Gluten-sensitive diners must ask staff and confirm cross-contamination handling.
vm_gojo_paradiso.vegan_detail: Omnivore Middle Eastern/Mediterranean dining bar in a converted Kyoto machiya with marked plant-based dishes: hummus, falafel, baba ganoush, an Israeli plate, plus vegetable pasta and a sabich-style sandwich. English menu; staff help identify vegan choices. The kitchen also serves egg (shakshuka) and meat (fried chicken), so it is not a vegan kitchen — confirm preparation when ordering.

vm_izusen_sagano.gf_detail: Not gluten-friendly. Wheat-based soy sauce and fu (wheat gluten) are essential, core ingredients in Izusen's cooking and cannot be modified, per InsideKyoto. Tempura batter and other course components also typically contain wheat flour. A celiac or strictly gluten-free diner should not rely on this restaurant; there is no dedicated GF menu or accommodation noted.
vm_izusen_sagano.vegan_detail: Izusen serves shojin ryori (Buddhist temple vegetarian cuisine) that is entirely plant-based, with no meat or fish. As traditional shojin, it also excludes the five pungent vegetables (onion, garlic, etc.). The signature is "teppatsu shojin ryori" — a multi-course set served in stacked red-lacquer iron bowls, featuring seasonal vegetables, sesame tofu (goma-dofu), tofu fritters, vegetable tempura, simmered and vinegared dishes, and matcha with Japanese sweets to finish. Set menus (Ran ¥2,600, Hana ¥3,300, Housenka ¥3,500, Ayame ¥3,800) change with the season. Note: authentic shojin does not use fish or katsuo dashi — broth is plant-based (kombu/shiitake) — but confirm directly if you have a strict requirement.

vm_taro_bakery.gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The bakery's identity is built on domestic (Hokkaido) wheat and natural-yeast wheat bread, so wheat is in essentially everything and the kitchen handles flour throughout. No dedicated gluten-free or wheat-free line was confirmed by reliable sources. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should treat this as unsafe.
vm_taro_bakery.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore bakery, but plant-based options are a core part of what they make. The base/plain bread (and a number of the daily loaves) is genuinely vegan: it is made from just four ingredients — Hokkaido domestic wheat, natural (wild) yeast, salt and water — with no butter, vegetable oil, milk, eggs or sugar, slow-fermented 15–18 hours. The owners say they put out "egg-free, dairy-free, sugar-free" items every day specifically for vegans and people with allergies. Seasonal sandwiches use pesticide-free Ukyo-grown vegetables (burdock, lotus root, shiitake, onion), but note that other pastries/sweets on the shelf may contain dairy/egg, so ask which specific items are plant-based on the day.

vm_yume_ya.gf_detail: The defining draw per the VegeMap listing is "gluten- and dairy-free doughnuts," so at least the doughnuts are made gluten-free. This is not a confirmed fully-dedicated gluten-free kitchen, and there is no information on cross-contamination controls, shared equipment, or whether other items contain wheat. Celiac diners should confirm preparation directly with staff.
vm_yume_ya.vegan_detail: Listed by Japan VegeMap as offering vegan option(s). The standout item is gluten- and dairy-free doughnuts paired with house-roasted coffee, which suggests plant-based-friendly baked goods. However, the VegeMap listing carries no menu detail and is flagged by VegeMap itself as unverified, so exactly which items are fully vegan (no eggs/honey) is not confirmed. A diner should tell staff their requirements and confirm each item before ordering.

vm_hakko_shokudo_kamoshika_.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or GF information is published. The kitchen's seasonings center on traditional Japanese fermented products — soy sauce (醤油), miso, and amazake. Their dressing ingredient list includes soy sauce, and standard Japanese shoyu and most miso contain wheat, so dishes should be assumed to contain gluten unless confirmed. Rice and many pickles are naturally gluten-free, but cross-use of wheat-containing soy sauce/miso is likely. A celiac/gluten-sensitive diner must ask directly; safety cannot be assumed.
vm_hakko_shokudo_kamoshika_.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The flagship dish, the "8-kinds fermented set meal" (発酵8種定食), contains fish: mackerel heshiko, pufferfish pickled in rice bran, and konjac dressed with fish sauce. However the kitchen leans heavily on plant-based fermentation, so vegan/plant-based choices exist: koji-natto served in/over deep-fried tofu, fermented vegetable pickles (nukazuke), white-miso soup, and salads dressed with their house brown-rice amazake dressing (explicitly labeled vegan/植物性: brown-rice amazake, rapeseed oil, onion, vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, plum, salt). A vegan diner should ask staff to omit the fish/fish-sauce components of the set or order plant-based items à la carte. White miso soup may use dashi — confirm it is kombu-based and not bonito.

vm_orto.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu is course-based and bread/pasta feature heavily (e.g. shiitake bread, grilled negi bread, maitake pasta), so wheat is prevalent. The restaurant asks guests to declare all allergies and dislikes in advance of the visit (it cannot accommodate requests after arrival), which means a gluten-free adaptation would have to be negotiated when reserving. Whether soy sauce is wheat-free and whether any dashi is used in the vegan course are not stated — confirm directly. Treat GF as ask/by-arrangement only.
vm_orto.vegan_detail: ORTO is an omnivore innovative restaurant (its standard tasting course features fish, octopus, duck and house-made bacon) but it offers a dedicated, fully vegan multi-course menu and a separate vegetarian course — one of the few high-end Kyoto restaurants with a genuine 100% vegan set course. The kitchen defines vegan as no meat, fish, shellfish, dairy, eggs or honey. The cooking centers on seasonal Kyoto vegetables (kyoyasai), edible flowers and herbs, with restrained salt and oil. Per VegeMap, vegan reservations are accepted at lunch only and must be arranged in advance — you cannot order the vegan course on the spot. Always reserve the vegan/vegetarian course ahead and state your needs clearly when booking.

vm_kerala.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program documented. Rice dishes (saffron/basmati rice) and rice-flour dosas are naturally wheat-free, but naan, papad, pakora batter and some curry thickeners may contain wheat, and shared cookware means cross-contact is likely. No information on dedicated fryers or wheat-free soy sauce. A GF-sensitive or celiac diner must ask staff directly about each dish.
vm_kerala.vegan_detail: Omnivore Indian restaurant with a strong vegetable focus rooted in Kerala/Ayurvedic cuisine, offering many plant-based choices. Reviewers report a range of vegan-adaptable dishes: tofu curry, chana masala, aloo gobi, dal, vegetable pakoras, and masala dosa. Vegan items are not labeled on the menu, but English-speaking staff can advise which dishes can be made vegan by omitting dairy and eggs (and confirm whether chutneys/accompaniments are vegan). Naan and many breads contain wheat; the soybean/lotus-root curry and rice-based dosas are good plant-based picks. Confirm ghee, cream (used in some curries), and dairy in breads when ordering.

vm_pippara.gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Wheat chapati and battered fried snacks are core to the set; no GF program or dedicated fryer. A GF diner could potentially eat turmeric rice with a vegetable curry, but cross-contamination from shared wheat handling and frying makes it unsuitable for strict celiacs without careful confirmation.
vm_pippara.vegan_detail: Omnivore Indian/Nepalese curry counter, not vegan-dedicated. The standard set includes fish (often mackerel, varies daily) and dairy (lassi, yogurt, paneer snack, possibly ghee). It is vegetarian-friendly: the plate contains several plant-based vegetable and legume curries (potato-tomato, chana/chickpea). A vegan should explicitly ask to skip fish/meat and all dairy (no yogurt/lassi, confirm oil vs ghee, check the coconut chutney). Onion/garlic likely present. Vegan is possible-on-request, not a fixed menu.

vm_sushizen.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. Standard sushi-shop soy sauce (shoyu) typically contains wheat, and there is no indication of tamari or wheat-free options. The rolls and rice are not documented as gluten-free. A gluten-sensitive diner should ask in advance and bring their own GF soy sauce; cross-contact in a busy fish-prep kitchen is likely.
vm_sushizen.vegan_detail: This is a traditional seafood sushi shop, not a vegan/vegetarian establishment. The menu is overwhelmingly fish-based (chirashi, nigiri, tekka/anago/saba). The only plant-based maki are the cucumber roll (kappa-maki, ¥900) and the plum-shiso roll (ume-shiso-maki, ¥900), which are vegan; shinko-maki (pickled radish roll, ¥900) is also plant-based. Futomaki and tamago-maki contain egg and are not vegan. There is no published confirmation of whether the sushi rice/vinegar, pickled radish, or rolls use animal- or wheat-free seasonings, so a strict vegan should ask directly. A vegan diner would have very few choices here.

vm_premarche_gelateria_kyot.gf_detail: The shop states none of its gelato contains gluten-containing ingredients, and a gluten-free option is offered. Standard cones/cups should be confirmed, but they sell an organic mochi (rice-based) gluten-free waffle cup (about ¥100) specifically for guests with wheat allergies. Because gelato itself is gluten-free, the main GF concern is the serving vessel — request the mochi gluten-free cup. No in-house wheat/soy-sauce cooking is involved (this is a dessert shop), reducing gluten cross-contact risk.
vm_premarche_gelateria_kyot.vegan_detail: A natural/organic gelato specialist offering 40+ flavors at any time, including one of Japan's largest selections of vegan and non-dairy ("non-milk") gelato. The menu mixes dairy gelato with clearly-labeled vegan options: pure vegan gelato (no animal products, honey or added oils) and fruit-and-sugar sorbetto, plus signature vegan flavors such as premium Sicilian pistachio and organic olive oil & almond, and non-milk flavors like black sugar with organic kinako. Diners pick from a rotating case, so vegan availability is genuine and broad but not the whole menu — ask staff to point out the vegan vs. milk flavors.

vm_arash_s_kitchen.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program confirmed. Naturally GF-leaning items exist (Persian rice dishes, dal, falafel is typically chickpea-based, hummus, eggplant dips), but falafel can contain wheat flour as a binder and naan/pizza/some appetizers contain wheat. Tabelog lists "gluten-free options" as a tag but no preparation/cross-contamination detail is published, and a shared kitchen frying naan/breaded items is likely. Celiac travelers should call ahead (+81 80-5366-5573) to confirm each dish. No information on soy sauce or dashi (this is Persian/Indian cuisine, so Japanese wheat soy sauce/dashi are unlikely to be central, but unconfirmed).
vm_arash_s_kitchen.vegan_detail: Omnivore Halal restaurant with a meat-forward kabab/curry menu but a solid roster of plant-based dishes. Vegan-likely items include falafel, hummus tahini, baba ghanoush, kashk-e-bademjan (eggplant; note kashk is a fermented whey product so ask), salad shirazi, dal (lentil) curry, and several Persian rice dishes (basmati, zereshk polo, baghali polo). Some "vegetarian" items contain dairy (palak paneer, mast-o-khiar yogurt dip, vegetarian pizza cheese), so distinguish vegan from vegetarian when ordering. The menu does not visibly label dishes vegan/vegetarian, so confirm dairy/egg/butter (ghee) per dish; the kitchen advertises egg-free and dairy-free accommodation.

vm_que_pasa.gf_detail: Not a gluten-free-friendly spot. The core menu is burritos (flour/wheat tortillas) and the kitchen makes heavy use of wheat, so there is no documented gluten-free offering or accommodation. Corn-tortilla tacos may be naturally wheat-free, but no dedicated GF preparation, fryer, or cross-contamination control is documented; a celiac diner should not assume safety. Ask directly before relying on any item.
vm_que_pasa.vegan_detail: Omnivore Mexican burrito shop with solid vegan choices. The vegetarian burrito and vegetarian taco can be made vegan by asking for no cheese and no sour cream; bean burritos, tacos, nachos, and three fresh salsas are available with modifications. The owner understands veganism and has made custom tofu and shiitake-mushroom burritos on request. There is also an all-vegan menu run roughly once a month. Filling proteins like beef and pork are NOT vegan, so always specify vegan / no cheese / no sour cream when ordering.

vm_da_maeda.gf_detail: The official site and dietary directories list a gluten-free menu as available, but the core of the restaurant is Italian (wood-fired pizza, pasta, breaded items), so wheat is central to the standard offerings and gluten-free dishes must be requested. There is no published statement confirming a dedicated gluten-free preparation area, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce, so a celiac diner should call ahead (075-465-5258) and confirm cross-contamination handling in person. Treat as gluten-free-by-request, not dedicated.
vm_da_maeda.vegan_detail: da Maeda is an organic Italian restaurant in a renovated Kyoto townhouse that offers a clearly marked vegan and vegetarian menu alongside its regular dishes — it is not a fully plant-based kitchen. Vegan plant-based plates include a soy patty in porcini red-wine demiglace, a breaded soy cutlet with almond-caper sauce, chickpea croquettes, and a vegetable curry/stew with pita, plus a vegetarian pizza. Vegetables come largely from the owner's own organic farm in Nantan, Kyoto. Staff speak English and ask you to state vegan/vegetarian needs and allergies when ordering so they can guide the menu. The same family runs a separate, fully-vegan concept, Lapis (vegan sushi kaiseki), by reservation at the same machiya.

vm_bean_s_goody.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The black bean coffee itself is naturally gluten-free (just brewed black soybeans), but food items such as the vegetable burger, sandwiches and baked goods are wheat-based. Whether any GF options exist is unconfirmed; a gluten-sensitive diner should ask directly or stick to the black bean coffee/tea and black bean jelly.
vm_bean_s_goody.vegan_detail: This is an omnivore machiya cafe (the sandwich menu includes ham & cheese, tuna and egg) but it offers several plant-based choices. The signature black bean coffee (黒豆珈琲) is made purely from Tanba black soybeans, is naturally caffeine-free and inherently plant-based. Cafe au lait can be made with soy milk instead of dairy. There is a vegetable burger, and the cafe has sold vegan baked goods from "Seiun Oyatsu" (星雲おやつ), which use no animal-derived ingredients. Confirm individual items (e.g. whether burger bun/sauce and any cakes contain egg/dairy) when ordering, as most desserts (ice cream, cakes) appear to be standard dairy-based.

vm_the_ritz_carlton_kyoto.gf_detail: La Locanda marks gluten-free items (GF) on its menu, and the hotel sources a 100% gluten-free amazake granola made locally for its breakfast — so GF awareness is real and staff handle allergy disclosures formally. However there is no dedicated GF kitchen or fryer documented; the Japanese outlets (tempura, dishes using soy sauce/dashi) carry wheat/gluten risk. GF diners should declare their needs at booking and confirm each dish; cross-contamination controls are not published.
vm_the_ritz_carlton_kyoto.vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The Ritz-Carlton Kyoto is a luxury hotel with several dining outlets, and vegan diners are accommodated on request. The clearest plant-based access is at the Italian restaurant La Locanda, whose menu explicitly marks items (VG) for vegan, (GF) for gluten-free and (LF) for lactose-free. Across outlets the hotel asks guests to inform their server of allergies/vegetarian-vegan needs and will customize dishes (reported examples include edamame, vegetable sushi, and miso soup). Dashi-free and wheat-free soy sauce status are not documented and must be confirmed per dish with the kitchen.

vm_asian_chample_foods_goya.gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The cuisine relies on soy sauce, miso, and fried items (tempura, croquettes), and there is no published statement on whether the soy sauce is wheat-free or whether a dedicated fryer is used. A celiac or gluten-sensitive diner should contact the restaurant directly and confirm ingredients in person.
vm_asian_chample_foods_goya.vegan_detail: An omnivore Okinawan/pan-Asian restaurant that clearly marks vegan and vegetarian dishes on its menu. Signature plant-based items include vegan taco rice (soy-meat with avocado instead of cheese), vegan banana tarte, spicy Vege Goreng, Nasi Campur / mixed seasonal vegetable plates with Okinawan seaweed, summer rolls, vegetable croquettes, and seaweed tempura. Staff proactively confirm whether cheese, egg or dashi are acceptable, so a strictly vegan meal is achievable but you should specify your needs when ordering since meat and animal-based Okinawan dishes are also served.

vm_yasai_dining_kusushi.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The menu includes clearly wheat-containing items (pizza), and as a Japanese izakaya it likely uses standard wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings. There is no indication of gluten-free accommodation; a celiac or gluten-sensitive diner would need to inquire in advance and should treat cross-contamination as unmanaged unless the kitchen confirms otherwise.
vm_yasai_dining_kusushi.vegan_detail: This is a vegetable-centric omnivore izakaya ("yasai dining"), not a vegan restaurant. Its concept is showcasing rare Kyoto vegetables and produce from named farmers nationwide, but the regular menu includes non-vegan items (e.g. cheese-tomato bakes, pizza topped with cheese) and it operates as an izakaya with wine. The vegan map listing notes vegan service is available "by advance reservation" only, so vegans should call ahead (075-212-4313) to arrange a plant-only course. No published confirmation of whether dashi, soy sauce, or other animal-derived seasonings are avoided in vegan requests, so confirm directly when booking.

vm_monpan_shokudo.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu. The signature dish, monpan, is a wheat flatbread, and the kitchen also serves yakisoba, pasta, noodles and Korean pancakes, so wheat is widely used. No information was found on whether the soy sauce is wheat-free or whether sauces are gluten-free. One reviewer reported the owner accommodated their allergies when handed an explanation written in Japanese, so individual accommodation may be possible, but a GF diner must communicate needs clearly (ideally in writing/Japanese) and verify each item.
vm_monpan_shokudo.vegan_detail: A homey omnivore eatery with extensive plant-based offerings. There are separate vegetarian and non-vegetarian menus, and vegan choices are clearly marked; staff will also veganize many dishes on request. Vegan-friendly items include vegan yakisoba, veggie/tofu monpan, buddha bowls, stir-fries, noodles, curry with rice, and jijimi (Korean pancake). VegeMap and HappyCow both tag it as "vegan options" rather than fully vegan, and meat is served in the same kitchen, so confirm each dish when ordering. No information confirming whether dashi or other animal stock is avoided in default broths — best to ask.
